title: Elevating the Status of Evangelion Analysis
display_title: Elevating the Status of Evangelion Analysis
wikitext:
[]: Elevating the Status of Evangelion Analysis Post by J_Faulkner » Sun Dec 27, 2009 1:33 pm Evangelion analysis by fans has often been perceived by anime fans as pompous, pseudo-intellectual and classic examples of overanalysis. Furthermore, the presence of Evangelion outside anime fans remains weak. Looking at Evageeks as an intellectual enterprise, this is a worrying state of affairs. It's time to elevate the status of Evangelion analysis. The recent releases of the Rebuild movies have thrust Evangelion back into the foreground in the anime community. There is no better time than now to try and spread the message of Evangelion. The purpose of this includes, but is not limited to, 1. Destroying the viewpoint that Evangelion has no depth. 2. Establishing Evangelion's legitimate links to existing philosophical and psychological theory. 3. Broadening the appeal of Evangelion not only within the anime community, but beyond, whilst ensuring people appreciate the underlying messages. To this end, I propose that: 1. The fandom tries to submit focussed analyses of Evangelion to journals and get them published. This establishes powerful evidence for the depth of Evangelion with respect to existing theory and studies, given that such papers will have the green light from experts in the field. In fact, a paper has already been written by S. Napier: "When the Machines Stop: Fantasy, Reality, and Terminal Identity in Neon Genesis Evangelion and Serial Experiments Lain" (abstract is here [url]http://www.depauw.edu/sfs/abstracts/a88.htm#Napier[/url]). OK, so it's just in the highly specialized Science Fiction Studies , but why not aim for broader and reputable journals? 2. It is not enough to elevate the status of Evangelion analysis by simply establishing its credentials within the intellectual community. There has to be a corresponding outreach program operating, and a viable option is to do this on the Internet. Witness the powerful "debates" on climate change on messageboards and blogs that have helped to shape public opinion. Look at the use of the Internet by Chinese activists when they influenced people to boycott Carrefour. Marvel at how the popularity of Susan Boyle soared due to a YouTube video. Be incredulous at Mayweather and Pacquiao fans arguing for their man online in the latest steroids debacle, and in the process attempting to influence public opinion. That is the power the Internet can have in promoting a cause. Thus, I propose Evageeks draw up crack squads of "missionaries" that go to targeted websites and spread the message of Evangelion. These missionaries will adhere by a strict code and will report back to the fandom at regular intervals to gauge success. After more than 10 yrs, it's time for Evangelion analysis to evolve to a higher level. More ideas and action points welcome. Remember that the Internet is a battleground of ideas, and the Evangelion fandom must not lose this battle, if it wishes to establish a long-lasting legacy.

[]: Post by ObsessiveMathsFreak » Sun Dec 27, 2009 2:28 pm This is an interesting proposal, but we do have to keep a few things in mind. Firstly, most of the analysis of Evangelion that takes place on forums concerns the "whats" of Evangelion rather than the "whys". That is, we often focus on trying to explain the story and piece apart the details rather than explain it in the context of psychoanalysis, with regard to the directors intent, or in relation to other works. I think most scholarly articles are more concerned with the latter type of discussion. Would they really be interested in extended analyses of the mechanics of Third Impact, Shinji and Asuka, or What Gendou Said to Ritsuko? That said, I think it would be nice to see Evangelion promoted as a notable work of art. Obviously, as an anime, there would be a lot of prejudice towards serious discussion of Evangelion. Hence the lack of any in depth analysis thus far. While academics like Napier have attempted to discuss the series, their largest problem has essentially been their lack of response; their theories are not responded to. There would also be the issue of none of us being recognised academics in any relevant field. Sometimes academia can be a very closed shop. In terms of publishing in "reputable journals", I think that game is best avoided. We'd be best off picking journals based on their audience, i.e. people who would actually read such articles. One idea that has crossed my mind a few times is to have a collection of short essays on NGE published in a book. Considering the difficulty of passing through the pearly gates of academia, and the limited audience therein, something like a published book would allow more freedom and be more accessible to general fans of the show and other anime fans. There are the core group which really should be focused on. With regard to the idea of internet "evangelism" of the show, while I'm not sure that sending out missionaries is such a good idea, you are correct that promotion of the show should be oe of the goals of this site. The question is how to go about this. Some of the things we do try involve the news and wiki sections of the site. The news section actually gets quite a bit of traffic which I think helps. The wiki section is not perhaps as good as it could be, but again serves some purpose in promoting the show and its mysteries. One idea we haven't tried which could be very good at promoting the show is do make some Evangelion video which could be watched on Youtube(V actually already does this on his Youtube Channel). I think at the present time Youtube typed videos are the best way to reach a wider audience. We could make videos discussing various things; long ones presenting established theories, shorter ones discussing some other aspects of the show, etc, etc. It's something which could be looked into. My views here are perhaps tangential to J_Faulkner's. I think he is speaking of reaching the wider academic/scholarly community, while I am still discussing an anime-type audience. Nevertheless, I think the two are related. Reaching academia without the core anime base would not create a lasting impression. Having a strong fan following on the other hand would create something which could attract the type of scholarly interest you are looking for. Perhaps. Anyway, these are only some suggestions. I think the idea of changing perception of the show is a worthy goal we should really look into further.

[]: Post by Merridian » Sun Dec 27, 2009 2:47 pm I'm not sure this is necessary. NGE/EoE already had a tremendous impact on the industry, part of the medium, and the extreme fandom ("otaku-dom"). The people who write it off as meaningless pretentious nonsense either lack the patience, time, and appreciation to better digest it, or they completely lack the capacity to understand it at all. Sites like EvaGeeks generally offers the undecided people better insight into the workings of the show, and occasionally convinces people that typcially fall into the first category that the show DOES have merit. The second group consists of pretentions haters that you'll find in any situation--and they're the dyed in the wool folks that usually protest the series' merit just to be noticed, especially since most of their actual attacks lack power & grounding. J_Faulkner wrote: 1. Destroying the viewpoint that Evangelion has no depth.

This establishes powerful evidence for the depth of Evangelion with respect to existing theory and studies, given that such papers will have the green light from experts in the field. In fact, a paper has already been written by S. Napier: "When the Machines Stop: Fantasy, Reality, and Terminal Identity in Neon Genesis Evangelion and Serial Experiments Lain" (abstract is here [url]http://www.depauw.edu/sfs/abstracts/a88.htm#Napier[/url]). That is, we often focus on trying to explain the story and piece apart the details rather than explain it in the context of psychoanalysis, with regard to the directors intent, or in relation to other works. I think most scholarly articles are more concerned with the latter type of discussion. Would they really be interested in extended analyses of the mechanics of Third Impact, Shinji and Asuka, or What Gendou Said to Ritsuko?

[]: Post by The Eva Monkey » Mon Dec 28, 2009 12:18 am A few problems come to mind, regarding this endeavor: Hideaki Anno: "Neon Genesis Evangelion" http://forum.evageeks.org/viewtopic.php?t=8358 "The World Is Watching", by Dennis Redmond http://forum.evageeks.org/viewtopic.php?t=1584 The Commentary of Evil http://wiki.evageeks.org/The_Geektionary_A-G#Commentary_of_Evil The Mythology of Evangelion http://wiki.evageeks.org/The_Geektionary_N-S#Platinum_Special_Features Neon Genesis Evangelion: The Unofficial Guide http://forum.evageeks.org/viewtopic.php?t=171 V http://forum.evageeks.org/profile.php?mode=viewprofile&u=1693

[]: Post by Merridian » Mon Dec 28, 2009 12:37 am J_Faulkner wrote: there may be people who simply haven't heard much about Evangelion but would be interested, and would appreciate it, if it was more aggressively marketed or if its intellectual depth was made more clear to them. These are the people who could be won over by my proposed strategy.
